amdp-hana-016.easyUnderstanding: Simple words mein samjho: Jab tum koi bada kaam karte ho — ghar banana mein architect pehle blueprint banata hai, detail mein likhta hai kaunsa kamra kahan hoga, kaunsa material use hoga. Technical design document wahi blueprint hai ABAP/HANA development ke liye. AMDP aur HANA Optimization ke liye technical design document mein yeh sab likhte hain: problem kya hai aur kyun HANA pushdown zaruri hai, kaunse tables/views use honge, AMDP class aur method ka naam aur structure, SQLScript logic ka outline, performance expectation, aur kaise test karenge. Yeh document team ke liye, future maintainers ke liye, aur client sign-off ke liye hota hai. Bina document ke aaj tum banao, kal koi aur aaye toh samjhe kaise? Isliye documentation ek professional discipline hai, optional nahi.
amdp-hana-016.hinglishMasterExplanation:
AMDP aur HANA Optimization technical design document karna ek structured process hai. Interview mein yeh bolna kafi nahi ki 'hum document karte hain' — kya document karte hain yeh batao. Yeh sections hote hain:

1. Requirement Summary: Business requirement kya hai — kaunsa report/process slow hai, kitna data volume hai, current performance baseline. Yeh stakeholders ke liye context set karta hai.

2. As-Is Analysis: Current ABAP code ya query kaise kaam karta hai, kahan bottleneck hai — SELECT count, data transfer size, runtime measurement (SE30/ST05). Quantify karo: '5 lakh rows application server pe aa rahi hain, runtime 4 minutes.'

3. Solution Approach: Kya use karenge — AMDP, CDS view, ya hybrid. Kyun — justify karo: 'Complex multi-step calculation AMDP se better suit hoti hai kyunki CDS declarative limit hai.' Table function use hoga ya direct AMDP call.

4. Object Design: AMDP class name (ZCL_XXX_AMDP), method names, IMPORTING/EXPORTING parameters with types, USING clause mein tables/views list, class hierarchy agar multiple methods hain.

5. SQLScript Logic Outline: High-level steps — Step 1: filter base data, Step 2: join with master, Step 3: aggregate, Step 4: apply CASE WHEN bucketing. Pseudo-code level, not full code. Key HANA functions used mention karo.

6. Performance Expectation: Expected improvement — '5 lakh rows se sirf aggregated 50 rows return hogi, expected runtime under 10 seconds.'

7. Authorization Design: Kaunse ABAP authority checks AMDP call se pehle honge, DCL agar CDS involved hai.

8. Transport Strategy: Objects ka sequence — dictionary types pehle, phir class, phir CDS agar table function use hua.

9. Test Strategy: Unit test data, expected results, performance test plan — SE30 comparison before/after.

10. Dependencies: External CDS views, HANA calculation views, custom dictionary types jo AMDP use karega.

Documentation tools: Word/Confluence for narrative, SE80/ADT screenshots for class structure, Excel for performance baseline comparison. Some projects use SAP Solution Manager for technical specs — SOLMAN ke change documents mein attach karte hain.
amdp-hana-016.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'For AMDP and HANA Optimization technical design, I document the requirement with current performance baseline — runtime, data volume, number of rows transferred. Then I specify the solution: AMDP class name, method signatures with parameter types, USING clause tables and views, and a step-by-step SQLScript logic outline covering key operations like JOINs, aggregations, and CASE WHEN bucketing. I include authorization design — which ABAP authority checks precede the AMDP call. Finally, test strategy with expected performance improvement and transport object sequence.'

Hinglish tip: Interview mein ek strong statement add karo: 'Documentation sirf paper ka kaam nahi hai — yeh future maintainer ke liye roadmap hai. AMDP mein SQLScript debug karna already mushkil hai, bina design doc ke maintenance nightmare ban jaata hai.' Yeh maturity show karta hai.
amdp-hana-016.codeExamples:
" === Technical Design Document — Code Section Example ===

" Object: ZCL_VENDOR_AGING_AMDP
" Method: GET_VENDOR_AGING
" Purpose: Vendor open item aging report with HANA pushdown
" Tables Used: BSIK, BKPF, LFA1
" Auth Check: F_LFB1_BUK in calling wrapper before AMDP

CLASS zcl_vendor_aging_amdp DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES: if_amdp_marker_hdb.
    TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_aging_result,
             lifnr TYPE lifnr,
             name1 TYPE name1,
             dmbtr TYPE dmbtr,
             age_days TYPE i,
             bucket TYPE char10,
           END OF ty_aging_result.
    TYPES: tt_aging_result TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF
                                ty_aging_result WITH DEFAULT KEY.
    METHODS: get_vendor_aging
      IMPORTING VALUE(iv_bukrs) TYPE bukrs
                VALUE(iv_gjahr) TYPE gjahr
      EXPORTING VALUE(et_result) TYPE tt_aging_result.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_vendor_aging_amdp IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD get_vendor_aging
      BY DATABASE PROCEDURE FOR HDB
      LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT OPTIONS READ-ONLY
      USING bsik bkpf lfa1.
    lt_open = SELECT i.lifnr, i.belnr,
                     i.dmbtr, i.bldat
              FROM bsik AS i
              WHERE i.bukrs = :iv_bukrs;
    et_result = SELECT o.lifnr,
                       COALESCE(v.name1,'?') AS name1,
                       o.dmbtr,
                       DAYS_BETWEEN(o.bldat,CURRENT_DATE) AS age_days,
                       CASE WHEN DAYS_BETWEEN(o.bldat,CURRENT_DATE) <= 30
                            THEN '0-30'
                            ELSE '30+' END AS bucket
              FROM :lt_open AS o
              LEFT JOIN lfa1 AS v ON o.lifnr = v.lifnr;
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.
amdp-hana-016.interviewScriptHinglish: HANA optimization ya AMDP ke liye Technical Design Document koi bhi code likhne se pehle architectural intent capture karta hai. Mandatory sections hain: business requirement, current state vs target state, class aur method signatures, USING clause dependencies, SQLScript logic flow pseudocode mein, authorization model, error handling strategy, transport sequence, performance baseline, aur test cases. Development ke liye ADT use karte hain lekin TDD ko Confluence ya SharePoint mein diagrams ke saath maintain karte hain. Peer review design issues catch karta hai jaise missing client filters ya inadequate authorization, code tak pahunchne se pehle. Business value kam defects, smoother audits, aur team members rotate hone par faster onboarding hai.
amdp-hana-016.interviewScriptEnglish: A Technical Design Document for HANA optimization or AMDP captures the architectural intent before any code is written. Mandatory sections include business requirement, current state vs target state, class and method signatures, USING clause dependencies, the SQLScript logic flow described in pseudocode, authorization model, error handling strategy, transport sequence, performance baseline, and test cases. We use ADT for development but maintain the TDD in Confluence or SharePoint with diagrams. Peer review catches design issues such as missing client filters or inadequate authorization before they reach code. The business value is fewer defects, smoother audits, and faster onboarding when team members rotate.

wordByWordSamjho.meaning: AMDP clause documented in TDD listing every dependent table or view
Method parameters captured in the signature section of the TDD
Section describing how AUTHORITY-CHECK is enforced in the ABAP wrapper
Recorded ST05 or PlanViz numbers used as success criteria
Ordered list of dependent objects that must move together

commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Transport failures occur when dependent CDS or tables are not part of the sequence
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: List every transparent table, CDS view, and called AMDP in the TDD with transport order

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Writing the SQLScript directly in TDD as runnable code
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: TDD becomes a brittle copy of the source and falls out of sync
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Capture pseudocode with logical steps and a link to the code repository

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Documenting performance expectations without baseline numbers
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: There is nothing to compare against during testing or post-go-live
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Capture the baseline ST05/PlanViz figures and target SLA in the TDD

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Treating peer review as a formality
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Misses authorization, NULL handling, and pushdown gaps that are hard to find later
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Use a structured AMDP review checklist signed off by another senior developer

followupAnswerBank.question: What sections are mandatory in a HANA optimization technical design document?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: HANA optimization technical design ke mandatory sections include karte hain: current runtime aur data volume ke saath performance baseline, solution approach justification, parameter types ke saath AMDP class aur method design, step-by-step processing ke saath SQLScript logic outline, ABAP authority checks specify karne wala authorization design, expected improvement metrics ke saath test strategy, aur transport object sequence.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Mandatory sections in a HANA optimization technical design include: performance baseline with current runtime and data volume, solution approach justification, AMDP class and method design with parameter types, SQLScript logic outline with step-by-step processing, authorization design specifying ABAP authority checks, test strategy with expected improvement metrics, and transport object sequence.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Humara architect har HANA optimization spec mein 'Before/After' table mandatory rakha tha — current runtime, rows transferred, expected runtime, expected rows returned. Yeh table management ko quickly convey karta tha ROI. Sign-off isi table pe milti thi.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Performance baseline skip karna — agar before measurement nahi hai toh after improvement prove kaise karoge? Aur authorization design skip karna — AMDP ke saath auth gap bahut common vulnerability hai jab design time pe document nahi kiya.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Technical Design — Performance Baseline Table (in doc):
" +-------------------+----------+----------+
" | Metric            | Before   | After    |
" +-------------------+----------+----------+
" | Runtime           | 4 min    | 8 sec    |
" | Rows transferred  | 500,000  | 50       |
" | Memory (App Srv)  | 2 GB     | <1 MB    |
" +-------------------+----------+----------+

" Object inventory table:
" +------------------------------+--------+
" | Object Name                  | Type   |
" +------------------------------+--------+
" | ZTT_VENDOR_AGING_RESULT      | DDIC   |
" | ZCL_VENDOR_AGING_AMDP        | Class  |
" | ZTF_VENDOR_AGING             | CDS TF |
" +------------------------------+--------+
" Transport sequence: DDIC -> Class -> CDS

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you document the USING clause dependencies in the design document?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: USING clause dependencies ko dependency table ke roop mein document karna chahiye jisme har object name, uska type — transparent table, CDS view, ya HANA calculation view — aur AMDP logic mein uska purpose listed ho. Yeh transport sequence drive karta hai: sab USING-clause objects AMDP class activate hone se pehle transport aur active hone chahiye. Design document mein explicitly transport order state karna chahiye target system mein activation failures prevent karne ke liye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: USING clause dependencies should be documented as a dependency table listing each object name, its type — transparent table, CDS view, or HANA calculation view — and its purpose in the AMDP logic. This drives the transport sequence: all USING-clause objects must be transported and activated before the AMDP class. The design document should explicitly state the transport order to prevent activation failures in the target system.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Ek project mein AMDP ek custom CDS interface view use karta tha. Transport sequence document mein nahi tha. Target system mein CDS view transport nahi hua pehle, AMDP activate nahi hua — emergency fix karna pada. Us ke baad humne har spec mein dependency diagram mandatory kiya.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Sirf USING clause copy-paste karna code se bina explaining karna — document mein explain karo each dependency kyun hai. Aur CDS views bhi USING mein jaate hain — sirf transparent tables nahi — yeh common gap hai documentation mein.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Dependency documentation example:

" USING Clause Objects for ZCL_VENDOR_AGING_AMDP:
" +-----------+------------------+---------------------------+
" | Object    | Type             | Purpose                   |
" +-----------+------------------+---------------------------+
" | BSIK      | Transparent Tbl  | Open vendor line items    |
" | BKPF      | Transparent Tbl  | Document header for dates |
" | LFA1      | Transparent Tbl  | Vendor master name        |
" | ZI_BUKRS  | CDS View         | Company code filter view  |
" +-----------+------------------+---------------------------+

" Transport sequence (documented):
" 1. ZI_BUKRS (CDS view)
" 2. ZTT_AGING_RESULT (DDIC table type)
" 3. ZCL_VENDOR_AGING_AMDP (ABAP class)
" Violating this sequence = activation failure in target

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you capture SQLScript logic in a design document without writing full code?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SQLScript logic ko design document mein full code ke bajaye numbered pseudo-code steps ke roop mein capture karte hain — example: Step 1 base table ko input parameters se filter karo, Step 2 master data ke saath join karo, Step 3 DAYS_BETWEEN use karke date calculations apply karo, Step 4 CASE WHEN se conditional bucketing apply karo, Step 5 final aggregated result return karo. Key HANA built-in functions noted hote hain. Yeh developer ko direction deta hai aur implementation flexibility allow karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: SQLScript logic in a design document is captured as numbered pseudo-code steps rather than full code — for example: Step 1 filter base table by input parameters, Step 2 join with master data, Step 3 apply date calculations using DAYS_BETWEEN, Step 4 apply conditional bucketing with CASE WHEN, Step 5 return final aggregated result. Key HANA built-in functions are noted. This gives the developer direction while allowing implementation flexibility.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Humara team US aur India mein split tha. Design doc mein step-outline se India team ne SQLScript implement kiya bina US lead se every line puchhe. Review ke time clear baseline tha ki logic sahi direction mein hai ya nahi — full code nahi, steps se comparison possible tha.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Design doc mein itna detail likhna ki code jaisa ho jaaye — yeh over-engineering hai aur design change flexibility kho deta hai. Pseudo-code steps kafi hain — actual implementation decisions developer pe chhodo.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Design Doc — SQLScript Logic Outline (pseudo-code):

" Method: GET_VENDOR_AGING
" Step 1: Filter BSIK by BUKRS = iv_bukrs, GJAHR = iv_gjahr
"         -> lt_open_items (tabular variable)
" Step 2: LEFT JOIN lt_open_items with LFA1 on LIFNR
"         Capture: LIFNR, NAME1, DMBTR, BLDAT
"         -> lt_enriched
" Step 3: Calculate age in days
"         DAYS_BETWEEN(BLDAT, CURRENT_DATE) -> AGE_DAYS
" Step 4: Apply aging bucket using CASE WHEN
"         0-30, 31-60, 61-90, 90+
" Step 5: Return lt_bucketed as et_result
"         ORDER BY AGE_DAYS DESC
" Key functions: DAYS_BETWEEN, COALESCE, CASE WHEN
" No LOOP — full set-based processing

followupAnswerBank.question: Which SAP tools do you use to document and review AMDP design?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: AMDP technical design documentation ke liye, main class structure screenshots aur method signature capture ke liye Eclipse mein ADT use karta hoon. ST05 aur SE30 optimization se pehle performance baseline measure karte hain. SAP Solution Manager change request se linked technical specification record karta hai. Confluence ya SharePoint design document team review ke liye host karta hai. AMDP class mein embed karne se pehle HANA Database Explorer SQLScript logic prototype aur validate karne ke liye use hota hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: For AMDP technical design documentation, I use ADT in Eclipse for class structure screenshots and method signature capture. ST05 and SE30 measure the performance baseline before optimization. SAP Solution Manager records the technical specification linked to the change request. Confluence or SharePoint hosts the design document for team review. HANA Database Explorer is used to prototype and validate SQLScript logic before embedding it in the AMDP class.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Ek client ke paas SAP Solution Manager mandatory tha — har change ki technical spec SOLMAN mein attach karni padti thi. Humne ADT se class screenshots, ST05 se performance report, aur Word document mein complete spec banaya — SOLMAN change document se link kiya. Audit ke time complete traceability thi.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Design document banana aur phir usse update na karna jab code change ho — stale documentation worse hai bez documentation se. Final implementation ke baad design doc sync karna important hai.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Documentation checklist (in design doc):
" [ ] Performance baseline captured via ST05 / SE30
" [ ] Class diagram in ADT screenshot attached
" [ ] Method signatures with all param types listed
" [ ] USING clause dependency table complete
" [ ] SQLScript logic outlined in numbered steps
" [ ] Authorization design: ABAP checks listed
" [ ] Transport sequence documented
" [ ] Test cases with expected results defined
" [ ] SOLMAN change document linked (if applicable)
" [ ] Peer review sign-off recorded

" ST05 trace: Tools -> ABAP Workbench -> Test -> SQL Trace
" SE30: System -> Utilities -> Runtime Analysis
" ADT: right-click class -> Open With -> Class Editor

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you document performance expectations in a HANA optimization spec?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Performance expectations ko quantified targets ke roop mein document karna chahiye — generic statements nahi. Document mein state karna chahiye SE30 ke through measured current runtime, ST05 ke through measured current rows transferred, aur dono ke liye expected post-optimization values. Example: current 500,000 rows 4 minutes mein transferred, expected 50 aggregated rows under 15 seconds mein. Yeh targets performance testing ke acceptance criteria form karte hain aur management ko ROI clearly communicate karte hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Performance expectations should be documented as quantified targets — not generic statements. The document should state current runtime measured via SE30, current rows transferred measured via ST05, and expected post-optimization values for both. For example: current 500,000 rows transferred in 4 minutes, expected 50 aggregated rows in under 15 seconds. These targets form the acceptance criteria for performance testing and communicate ROI clearly to management.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Management ne poochha: HANA optimization mein kitna invest karna hai aur kya milega? Design doc mein 'Before/After' table thi — runtime 4 min to 8 sec, rows 5 lakh to 50. Unhone seedha approve kiya. Numbers se business case banta hai, words se nahi.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Performance target likha aur phir test nahi kiya — design doc mein commitment hai. Post-implementation ST05/SE30 run karo aur actual vs expected compare karo. Yeh document karo bhi — post-implementation review section mein.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Performance Expectations Table (in design doc):
" +----------------------+-----------+------------------+
" | Metric               | Current   | Target (Post-Opt)|
" +----------------------+-----------+------------------+
" | Runtime (SE30)       | 240 sec   | < 15 sec         |
" | Rows to App Server   | 500,000   | < 100 (agg)      |
" | DB Time %            | 15%       | > 85% (in HANA)  |
" | App Server Memory    | ~2 GB     | < 50 MB          |
" +----------------------+-----------+------------------+

" Measurement tools:
" Before: ST05 SQL trace -> total rows fetched
"         SE30 Runtime analysis -> time breakdown
" After: same tools, compare
" Acceptance criterion: all targets met = design approved

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle design changes during development of an AMDP?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: AMDP development ke dauran design changes ko code change karne se pehle design document update karke handle karte hain — document-first principle. Ek change log section track karta hai ki kya change hua, kyun, aur kab. Significant changes — new USING dependencies, parameter type changes, ya performance impact — stakeholder notification aur formal re-review require karte hain. Minor logic refinements team ke andar discuss karte hain aur design doc ko actual implementation ke saath sync mein rakhne ke liye update karte hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Design changes during AMDP development are handled by first updating the design document before changing the code — document-first principle. A change log section tracks what changed, why, and when. Significant changes — new USING dependencies, parameter type changes, or performance impact — require stakeholder notification and formal re-review. Minor logic refinements are discussed within the team and the design doc is updated to stay in sync with the actual implementation.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Development ke beech pata chala ki ek aur table add karna padega USING clause mein — transport sequence change hota. Design doc mein change log update kiya, lead ko inform kiya, transport sequence revised kiya. Bina doc update ke transport target mein fail ho sakta tha — dependency miss hoti.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Code change karna aur design doc update karna bhoolna — weeks baad jab koi review kare toh doc aur code mismatch confusing hota hai. 'Living document' — code ke saath evolve karna padta hai.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Change Log section in design document:
" +--------+------------+--------------------------------+
" | Ver    | Date       | Change Description             |
" +--------+------------+--------------------------------+
" | v1.0   | 2025-01-10 | Initial design approved        |
" | v1.1   | 2025-01-18 | Added LFA1 to USING clause;    |
"         |            | transport seq updated          |
" | v1.2   | 2025-01-25 | EXPORTING param type changed   |
"         |            | to include NAME1 field;        |
"         |            | DDIC type ZTT_VENDOR_AGING     |
"         |            | updated accordingly            |
" +--------+------------+--------------------------------+
" Rule: doc updated BEFORE code change, not after

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the role of peer review in AMDP technical design?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: AMDP technical design ka peer review technical correctness aur completeness dono validate karta hai. Technical review checks: kya AMDP sahi approach hai ya CDS suffice karega, kya sab USING dependencies listed hain, kya authorization design sound hai, kya parameter types correct hain. Completeness review checks: sab mandatory sections present hain, performance baseline captured hai, transport sequence documented hai, test strategy defined hai. Design time pe pakde gaye issues development ke dauran ya baad mein paaye gaye issues se kahin sasta fix hote hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Peer review of an AMDP technical design validates both technical correctness and completeness. Technical review checks: is AMDP the right approach or would CDS suffice, are all USING dependencies listed, is the authorization design sound, are parameter types correct. Completeness review checks: all mandatory sections present, performance baseline captured, transport sequence documented, test strategy defined. Issues caught at design time are far cheaper to fix than issues found during or after development.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Mere design review mein senior developer ne pakda ki main ek CDS view ko USING mein miss kar raha tha — woh view HANA calculation view wrap karta tha aur separately transport hona tha. Bina review ke target system mein activation fail hoti. Us ek comment ne ek production incident save kiya.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Self-review se kaam nahi chalta AMDP design mein — SQLScript logic aur dependency management mein subtle gaps hote hain jo fresh eyes hi pakad sakte hain. Formal review step skip karna costly hota hai.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Peer Review Checklist (attach to design doc):
" Technical Correctness:
" [ ] AMDP approach justified over CDS alternative
" [ ] All USING clause objects identified correctly
" [ ] No ABAP statements planned inside AMDP body
" [ ] Authorization: ABAP checks in wrapper confirmed
" [ ] OPTIONS READ-ONLY specified (if read-only logic)
" [ ] Parameter types are DDIC-based, not local types

" Completeness:
" [ ] Performance baseline numbers present
" [ ] SQLScript logic steps outlined
" [ ] Transport sequence listed and correct
" [ ] Test strategy with expected results
" [ ] Change log initialized

" Reviewer sign-off: _________________ Date: _______

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you document authorization design for an AMDP?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Kyunki AMDP ABAP AUTHORITY-CHECK statements contain nahi kar sakti, authorization design ko design stage pe explicitly document karna padta hai. Document specify karta hai ABAP wrapper method jo AMDP call karta hai aur har AUTHORITY-CHECK list karta hai — object name, field, aur expected value — jo AMDP call se pehle execute hona chahiye. Agar CDS table function AMDP wrap karta hai, @AccessControl annotation aur DCL access policy bhi documented hain. Yeh implementation ke dauran authorization gaps overlooked hone se rokta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Since AMDP cannot contain ABAP AUTHORITY-CHECK statements, the authorization design must be explicitly documented at the design stage. The document specifies the ABAP wrapper method that calls the AMDP and lists every AUTHORITY-CHECK — object name, field, and expected value — that must execute before the AMDP call. If a CDS table function wraps the AMDP, the @AccessControl annotation and DCL access policy are also documented. This prevents authorization gaps being overlooked during implementation.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Security review mein ek AMDP tha jisme calling code mein authority check tha lekin design doc mein mention nahi tha. New developer ne code change kiya, wrapper method restructure ki, authority check accidentally remove ho gayi. Design doc mein explicit authorization section hota toh code review mein pakad mein aata.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Authorization design section skip karna yeh assume karke ki 'koi toh check karega' — yeh assumption dangerous hai. Explicit document karo: exactly which object, which field, which value — reviewers aur future maintainers ke liye clear hai.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Authorization Design Section (in design doc):

" Calling Wrapper Method: ZCL_VENDOR_REP=>GET_AGING_REPORT
" Authority Checks BEFORE AMDP call:
" AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'F_LFB1_BUK'
"   FIELD 'BUKRS' ID iv_bukrs
"   FIELD 'ACTVT' ID '03'.
" IF sy-subrc <> 0 -> raise cx_no_authority.

" AMDP itself: NO authority-check inside
" (SQLScript = HANA layer, no ABAP auth available)

" If CDS Table Function used:
" @AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
" DCL file: ZTF_VENDOR_AGING.dcl
" Access policy: restrict by BUKRS using
"   pfcg_auth(F_LFB1_BUK, BUKRS, ACTVT='03')

" Result: multi-layer auth = no gap possible

amdp-hana-017.easyUnderstanding: Simple words mein samjho: Socho tumhe ek naya room banana hai ghar mein. Contractor se poochho kitna time lagega — woh pehle dekhega: room kitna bada hai, kya material chahiye, kaun kaun kaam karega, koi mushkil wall hai kya. Phir estimate dega. AMDP aur HANA Optimization ka effort estimate karna bilkul aisa hi hai. Tum dekhte ho: problem kitna complex hai, data volume kitna hai, kaunse tables involve hain, team ka HANA experience kaisa hai, testing kitna hoga. In sab factors ke baad ek realistic effort estimate aata hai — days mein ya story points mein. Galat estimate se project delay hota hai — isliye careful analysis karo pehle.
amdp-hana-017.hinglishMasterExplanation:
AMDP aur HANA Optimization effort estimation ek structured approach hai. Interview mein yeh clearly explain karo:

1. Complexity Assessment: Simple AMDP — ek table, simple filter aur aggregation — 2-3 days. Medium — multiple tables join, CASE WHEN bucketing, custom DDIC types — 3-5 days. Complex — multi-step SQLScript, CDS table function integration, HANA calculation view access, complex date series — 5-10 days. Complexity categories clearly define karo.

2. Analysis Phase Effort: Current code analyze karna, ST05/SE30 baseline capture karna, bottleneck identify karna — yeh sab pehle hota hai. 0.5 to 1 day typically for analysis depending on code complexity.

3. Design Phase: Technical design document banana — class design, SQLScript logic outline, dependency mapping — 0.5 to 1 day. Complex scenarios mein zyada.

4. Development Phase: AMDP class banana, DDIC types banana, SQLScript likhna, CDS table function agar needed — yeh main effort hai. Complexity pe depend karta hai.

5. Testing Phase: Unit testing, performance testing (before/after ST05/SE30 comparison), integration testing, UAT support — typically 30-40% of dev effort. AMDP testing extra effort leta hai kyunki HANA debugging more complex hai.

6. Team Skill Factor: Team ko HANA/SQLScript experience hai toh estimate kam. Naya technology hoga toh learning curve add karo — 20-30% buffer.

7. Environment Factor: HANA system available hai, properly configured hai, HANA Studio/Database Explorer access hai — agar nahi toh setup time add karo.

8. Authorization and Transport: Auth design, DCL agar involved, transport sequence testing — extra 0.5 day.

9. Documentation: Design doc, code comments, test evidence — 0.5 to 1 day.

10. Risk Buffer: AMDP mein unexpected SQLScript issues, HANA version compatibility, data quality issues in performance testing — 10-20% buffer always add karo.

Typical estimates:
- Simple AMDP optimization: 3-4 days total
- Medium AMDP with CDS table function: 5-7 days total
- Complex multi-method AMDP with full test suite: 8-12 days total
amdp-hana-017.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'For AMDP and HANA Optimization effort estimation, I start with complexity assessment — simple single-table aggregation is 3-4 days total including design, development, testing, and documentation. Medium complexity with multi-table joins and CDS table function is 5-7 days. Complex scenarios with multi-step SQLScript, calculation view integration, or multiple AMDP methods run 8-12 days. I add a skill factor — if the team is new to SQLScript, 20-30% buffer. Testing gets 30-40% of development effort because AMDP debugging is more involved than standard ABAP. Hinglish tip: Interviewer impress hota hai jab tum testing effort separately call out karo: 'AMDP testing ABAP testing se zyada effort leta hai — HANA Database Explorer mein SQLScript debug karna, ST05 before/after compare karna, data volume testing — yeh sab estimate mein include karna zaroori hai warna project delay hota hai.' Yeh shows practical experience.
amdp-hana-017.codeExamples:
" === Effort Estimation Reference — Code Complexity Indicators ===

" COMPLEXITY LEVEL 1 — Simple (3-4 days total)
" Single table, basic filter + aggregation, standard types
METHOD simple_amdp
    BY DATABASE PROCEDURE FOR HDB
    LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT OPTIONS READ-ONLY
    USING bseg.
  et_result = SELECT bukrs, SUM(dmbtr) AS total
              FROM bseg WHERE gjahr = :iv_gjahr
              GROUP BY bukrs;
ENDMETHOD.

" COMPLEXITY LEVEL 2 — Medium (5-7 days total)
" Multi-table join, CASE WHEN, DDIC types, date functions
METHOD medium_amdp
    BY DATABASE PROCEDURE FOR HDB
    LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT OPTIONS READ-ONLY
    USING bsik bkpf lfa1.
  lt_open = SELECT i.lifnr, i.dmbtr,
                   DAYS_BETWEEN(i.bldat, CURRENT_DATE) AS age
            FROM bsik AS i WHERE i.bukrs = :iv_bukrs;
  et_result = SELECT o.lifnr, v.name1, o.dmbtr, o.age,
               CASE WHEN o.age <= 30 THEN '0-30'
                    WHEN o.age <= 60 THEN '31-60'
                    ELSE '60+' END AS bucket
             FROM :lt_open AS o
             LEFT JOIN lfa1 AS v ON o.lifnr = v.lifnr;
ENDMETHOD.

" COMPLEXITY LEVEL 3 — Complex (8-12 days total)
" Multiple methods, CDS table function, calculation view,
" custom exception handling, full test suite
" -> Add 20-30% if team new to SQLScript
" -> Add 0.5 day for HANA environment setup verification
amdp-hana-017.interviewScriptHinglish: AMDP effort estimation complexity, data volume, dependency depth, aur testing rigor consider karta hai. Ek simple read-only AMDP ek table aur basic aggregation ke saath one to two days le sakta hai; ek complex multi-step AMDP jo doosre AMDPs ko call kare, Fiori ke liye CDS Table Function ke saath integrate ho, aur extensive parallel testing chahiye, woh five to seven days le sakta hai. Hum effort ko design, development, unit testing, aur integration plus performance testing mein break karte hain. Story points agile teams mein relative sizing ke liye useful hain; fixed-bid projects ke liye calendar days better kaam karte hain. 20 percent buffer HANA-specific tuning iterations ke liye accommodate karta hai. Business value realistic timelines aur predictable delivery hai.
amdp-hana-017.interviewScriptEnglish: AMDP effort estimation considers complexity, data volume, dependency depth, and testing rigor. A simple read-only AMDP with one table and basic aggregation might take one to two days; a complex multi-step AMDP that calls other AMDPs, integrates with a CDS Table Function for Fiori, and requires extensive parallel testing can take five to seven days. We break effort into design, development, unit testing, and integration plus performance testing. Story points are useful for relative sizing in agile teams; calendar days work better for fixed-bid projects. A 20 percent buffer accommodates HANA-specific tuning iterations. The business value is realistic timelines and predictable delivery.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you estimate testing effort for AMDP optimization?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: AMDP testing effort standard ABAP se zyada hota hai kyunki yeh multiple dimensions involve karta hai: boundary data ke saath SQLScript logic unit testing, full production-scale data volumes ke saath performance testing ST05/SE30 before aur after compare karte hue, old aur new code parallel run karke aur outputs compare karke correctness validation, calling ABAP layer se integration testing, aur OData/Fiori testing agar CDS table function AMDP wrap karta hai. Main AMDP scenarios mein development effort ka 35-40% testing ke liye allocate karta hoon.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: AMDP testing effort is higher than standard ABAP because it involves multiple dimensions: unit testing SQLScript logic with boundary data, performance testing with full production-scale data volumes comparing ST05/SE30 before and after, correctness validation by running old and new code in parallel and comparing outputs, integration testing from the calling ABAP layer, and OData/Fiori testing if a CDS table function wraps the AMDP. I allocate 35-40% of development effort for testing in AMDP scenarios.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Ek optimization mein hum confident the ki AMDP faster hoga — but correctness testing mein pata chala HANA mein NULL handling ka behavior ABAP se alag tha. COALESCE add karna pada. Agar correctness test skip kiya hota toh production mein data discrepancy hoti. Isliye correctness testing mandatory hai.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Sirf performance test karna aur correctness test skip karna — HANA NULL handling, date format differences, aur rounding behavior ABAP se slightly differ kar sakta hai. Dono test karo — speed aur correctness.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Testing effort breakdown for AMDP optimization:

" 1. Unit Test (SQLScript logic validation)
"    - Sample 100-500 rows
"    - Run in HANA DB Explorer directly
"    - Verify: JOINs correct, CASE WHEN buckets right
"    Effort: 0.5-1 day

" 2. Correctness Validation
"    - Run OLD ABAP code: result set A
"    - Run NEW AMDP code: result set B
"    - Compare A vs B: ASSERT_EQUALS in test class
"    Effort: 0.5-1 day

" 3. Performance Test
"    - SE30 before AMDP, SE30 after AMDP
"    - ST05 SQL trace: rows transferred before vs after
"    - Full prod volume data required
"    Effort: 0.5 day

" 4. Integration Test: calling code -> AMDP -> result
"    Effort: 0.5 day
" Total testing: ~35-40% of dev effort

followupAnswerBank.question: What factors increase effort estimate for AMDP development?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: AMDP effort estimate badhane wale factors include karte hain: team ki SQLScript se unfamiliarity 20-30% learning curve buffer add karti hai, HANA Database Explorer access ki kami environment setup require karti hai, complex SQLScript logic jaise date series generation ya iterative calculations, multiple chained AMDP methods, OData exposure ke liye CDS table function integration, custom DDIC type creation, COALESCE aur NULL handling require karne wale data quality issues, aur multiple optimization rounds demand karne wali strict performance SLA requirements.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Factors that increase AMDP effort estimate include: team unfamiliarity with SQLScript adding 20-30% learning curve buffer, lack of HANA Database Explorer access requiring environment setup, complex SQLScript logic like date series generation or iterative calculations, multiple chained AMDP methods, CDS table function integration for OData exposure, custom DDIC type creation, data quality issues requiring COALESCE and NULL handling, and strict performance SLA requirements demanding multiple optimization rounds.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Ek project mein team ne pehli baar AMDP use kiya. Estimate kiya tha 5 days — actual 8 days laga. Extra time kahan gaya: SQLScript syntax errors pehle 2 days mein, HANA Database Explorer setup, aur NULL handling issues test mein. Us ke baad hum naye team ke liye hamesha 25% learning buffer add karte hain.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: HANA environment availability assume karna without verification — kabhi kabhi dev system mein HANA Database Explorer install nahi hota ya access restricted hai. Pehle check karo, estimate mein environment setup time add karo agar needed.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Effort Multiplier Reference:

" Base estimate (simple AMDP): 3 days

" Factor 1: Team new to SQLScript
"   +25% buffer = 3 * 1.25 = 3.75 days -> round to 4 days

" Factor 2: No HANA Database Explorer access
"   +0.5 day for environment setup

" Factor 3: CDS Table Function needed
"   +1 day (DDL, implementation, OData test)

" Factor 4: Custom DDIC types needed
"   +0.5 day per complex structure

" Factor 5: Strict perf SLA (e.g. must be < 5 sec)
"   +1 day extra performance testing rounds

" Adjusted estimate example:
" Base 3 + learning 0.75 + env 0.5 + TF 1.0 = 5.25 days
" -> Communicate as 5-6 days with stated assumptions

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you estimate effort when optimizing existing ABAP code to AMDP?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Existing ABAP to AMDP optimization ke liye effort estimating teen phases involve karta hai: analysis — existing code padhna, baseline measurement ke liye ST05 aur SE30 run karna, exact bottleneck identify karna — typically 0.5 se 1 day. Conversion — ABAP logic ko SQLScript ke roop mein rewrite karna, jo complexity pe depend karta hai: simple aggregations quickly convert hote hain, lekin complex conditions wale nested LOOPs 2-3x zyada lete hain. Validation — old aur new code parallel run karke outputs compare karna — critical hai aur normal testing se 0.5 se 1 day extra add karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Estimating effort for existing ABAP to AMDP optimization involves three phases: analysis — reading the existing code, running ST05 and SE30 for baseline measurement, identifying the exact bottleneck — typically 0.5 to 1 day. Conversion — rewriting the ABAP logic as SQLScript, which depends on complexity: simple aggregations convert quickly, but nested LOOPs with complex conditions take 2-3x longer. Validation — running old and new code in parallel and comparing outputs — is critical and adds 0.5 to 1 day beyond normal testing.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Legacy code tha — 200 lines ABAP, multiple nested LOOPs, complex condition logic. Analysis took 1 day just to understand what it was doing. SQLScript conversion took 3 days — set-based equivalents dhundhe, CASE WHEN replace kiya nested IF ke liye. Validation mein 2 discrepancies mili — NULL handling aur rounding. Fix + retest 1 more day. Total: 5 days for medium-complex conversion.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: ABAP code directly SQLScript mein translate karna line by line — yeh anti-pattern hai. SQLScript mein set-based approach chahiye. Conversion sirf rewrite nahi — rethink karna padta hai. Isliye estimate mein analysis time properly account karo.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Existing ABAP (to be converted):
" SELECT * FROM bsik INTO TABLE lt_items
"   WHERE bukrs = iv_bukrs.
" LOOP AT lt_items INTO ls_item.
"   COLLECT ls_item INTO lt_by_vendor.  " aggregation in ABAP
" ENDLOOP.

" Conversion effort assessment:
" - Simple SELECT + COLLECT -> easy: 0.5 day
" - Nested LOOP + IF conditions -> medium: 1-2 days
" - LOOP with lookups (READ TABLE) -> medium: 1-2 days
" - Complex business logic in LOOP -> hard: 3+ days

" Result: AMDP equivalent
METHOD converted_amdp
    BY DATABASE PROCEDURE FOR HDB
    LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT OPTIONS READ-ONLY
    USING bsik.
  et_result = SELECT lifnr, SUM(dmbtr) AS total
              FROM bsik WHERE bukrs = :iv_bukrs
              GROUP BY lifnr;
ENDMETHOD.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you present effort estimates to a non-technical stakeholder?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Non-technical stakeholders ko AMDP effort estimates present karte waqt, main technical phases ko business outcomes mein translate karta hoon. 'Humein SQLScript likhna hai aur USING clauses configure karne hain' bolne ke bajaye, main bolta hoon 'hum 5 din current report bottleneck analyze karne, processing logic ko directly database pe run karne ke liye rebuild karne, aur validate karne mein lagayenge ki results correct hain aur report 4 minutes ke bajaye under 10 seconds mein chalti hai.' Main effort ko investment versus benefit ke roop mein frame karta hoon, technical tasks ke roop mein nahi.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: When presenting AMDP effort estimates to non-technical stakeholders, I translate technical phases into business outcomes. Instead of saying 'we need to write SQLScript and configure USING clauses,' I say 'we will spend 5 days analyzing the current report bottleneck, rebuilding the processing logic to run directly on the database, and validating that the results are correct and the report runs in under 10 seconds instead of 4 minutes.' I frame the effort as investment versus benefit, not as technical tasks.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Client manager ne poochha '5 din kyun lagte hain just to make it faster?' Humne explain kiya: 1 din purani code samajhne mein, 2 din naya database-level code likhne mein, 2 din test karne mein — ensure karna ki results correct hain aur performance target achieve hua. Unhone immediately agree kiya — clear breakdown se trust banta hai.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Estimate sirf development time dena aur analysis/testing hide karna — stakeholder expect karta hai agar 5 din bola toh kaam ho jaana chahiye. Agar testing 2 zyada din lete hain toh scope creep lagta hai. Sab phases upfront communicate karo.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Non-technical estimate presentation format:

" Topic: Vendor Aging Report Optimization
" Current State: Report takes 4 minutes, blocks users
" Solution: Move calculation to database layer
" Business Benefit: Under 10 seconds, any time of day

" Effort Breakdown (plain language):
" Phase 1 - Analysis: 1 day
"   Understand current report, measure performance
" Phase 2 - Development: 2.5 days
"   Build new database-level calculation logic
" Phase 3 - Testing: 1.5 days
"   Verify results match, confirm speed improvement
" Phase 4 - Documentation: 0.5 day
"   Update technical records
" ---
" Total: 5.5 days
" Risk: If data quality issues found, +1 day buffer
" Deliverable: Report running < 10 sec, validated

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you estimate effort for a CDS table function compared to a direct AMDP call?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Ek direct AMDP call class aur AMDP method create karne aur ABAP se call karne ki zarurat require karta hai — core development effort. CDS table function add karta hai: parameters aur return structure ke saath CDS DDL definition, BY DATABASE FUNCTION use karke implementing AMDP class se linking, aur end-to-end OData ya Fiori testing agar table function service ke roop mein exposed hai. CDS table function direct AMDP call ke compared mein total estimate mein approximately 1 se 1.5 days add karta hai, lekin CDS ecosystem ke through broader consumption enable karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: A direct AMDP call requires creating the class and AMDP method and calling it from ABAP — the core development effort. A CDS table function adds: the CDS DDL definition with parameters and return structure, linking to the implementing AMDP class using BY DATABASE FUNCTION, and end-to-end OData or Fiori testing if the table function is exposed as a service. CDS table function adds approximately 1 to 1.5 days to the total estimate compared to a direct AMDP call, but enables broader consumption through the CDS ecosystem.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: First project mein hum direct AMDP se chale the — estimate 4 days tha, delivered 4 days mein. Second phase mein Fiori exposure chahiye thi — CDS table function add kiya. Extra 1.5 days lage — DDL writing, BY DATABASE FUNCTION change, OData test. Estimate accurately predict kiya tha second time kyunki pehle experience tha.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: CDS table function ko direct AMDP ke same estimate mein assume karna — OData testing especially zyada time leta hai agar Fiori integration involved hai. Always separate karo estimation mein.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Direct AMDP — effort baseline
" Task list:
" 1. DDIC type ZTT_RESULT (0.5 day)
" 2. Class ZCL_X_AMDP with IF_AMDP_MARKER_HDB (0.5 day)
" 3. AMDP method BY DATABASE PROCEDURE (1 day)
" 4. Caller ABAP code update (0.5 day)
" 5. Testing (1 day)
" Total: ~3.5 days

" CDS Table Function — add to above:
" 6. CDS DDL: define table function ZTF_X (0.5 day)
" 7. Change method to BY DATABASE FUNCTION (0.5 day)
" 8. OData service test / Fiori smoke test (0.5 day)
" Additional: +1.5 days
" CDS TF total: ~5 days

" Decision rule:
" Fiori/OData needed? -> CDS table function, +1.5 days
" ABAP-only consumption? -> direct AMDP, save 1.5 days

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle estimation uncertainty in AMDP projects?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: AMDP projects mein estimation uncertainty single-point numbers ke bajaye range estimates provide karke manage hoti hai — example, 4 se 6 days depending on data quality aur HANA environment availability. Main estimate ke underlying assumptions explicitly state karta hoon: HANA Database Explorer accessible hai, team mein basic SQLScript familiarity hai, aur performance testing ke liye production data volumes available hain. Agar koi assumption false prove ho, main isko estimate revision require karne wali scope change ki tarah treat karta hoon silent absorb karne ke bajaye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Estimation uncertainty in AMDP projects is managed by providing range estimates rather than single-point numbers — for example, 4 to 6 days depending on data quality and HANA environment availability. I explicitly state assumptions that underpin the estimate: HANA Database Explorer is accessible, the team has basic SQLScript familiarity, and production data volumes are available for performance testing. If any assumption proves false, I treat it as a scope change requiring estimate revision rather than absorbing the overrun silently.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Client ne pushed kiya single-number estimate ke liye. Maine diya: '5 days agar HANA access available hai aur team ne SQLScript pehle use ki hai; 7 days agar nahi.' Client ne confirm kiya HANA access available hai, team experienced hai — estimate 5 days. Delivered 5 days mein. Range + assumptions = honest estimation.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Overconfident single-point estimate dena to please the client — agar actual mein zyada laga toh credibility kho dete ho. Range estimate with stated assumptions professional practice hai — client ko decision making ki power deta hai.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Estimation communication template:

" AMDP Optimization: Vendor Payment Aging Report

" Estimate Range: 5-7 days

" Assumptions (estimate valid only if all hold):
" A1: HANA Database Explorer accessible in dev system
" A2: Team has prior SQLScript experience
" A3: Production data volume available for perf testing
" A4: DDIC table type ZTT_VENDOR_AGING does not exist
"     (else -0.5 day)
" A5: No CDS table function needed (ABAP call only)
"     (if Fiori needed, +1.5 days)

" Risk: +1 day if data quality issues found in testing
" Risk: +0.5 day if HANA version < SPS08 (func limits)

" Lower bound (5 days): all assumptions hold, no risks
" Upper bound (7 days): 1-2 assumptions fail or 1 risk

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you use story points vs days for AMDP effort estimation?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Agile projects mein, main AMDP estimation ke liye team ne calibrate ki hui reference story ke against story points use karta hoon. Ek simple AMDP 3 points hai, CDS table function ke saath medium complexity 5-8 points hai, aur complex multi-method AMDP 8-13 points hai. Waterfall ya fixed-price projects mein, main team ki velocity use karke days mein convert karta hoon. Client communication ke liye, days zyada concrete hain — stakeholders '8 story points' se '5 days' ko better relate karte hain. Main jo bhi format project methodology require karti hai use karta hoon, same underlying complexity assessment ke saath.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: In Agile projects, I use story points for AMDP estimation relative to a reference story the team has calibrated against. A simple AMDP is 3 points, medium complexity with CDS table function is 5-8 points, and complex multi-method AMDP is 8-13 points. In waterfall or fixed-price projects, I convert to days using the team's velocity. For client communication, days are more concrete — stakeholders relate better to '5 days' than '8 story points.' I use whichever format the project methodology requires, with the same underlying complexity assessment.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Agile SAP project mein humne reference story rakhi thi: 'Simple ABAP report with one SELECT = 2 points, 3 days.' AMDP medium complexity calibrate kiya at 5 points = roughly 7-8 days at our velocity. Jab sprint planning mein AMDP story aati thi, team quickly reference story se compare karta tha. Consistent estimation across sprints.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Story points aur days ko mix karna stakeholder communication mein — ek hi estimate ek format mein do. Internally team story points use kare, client-facing communication days mein karo for clarity.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Story Point Reference Table (team calibrated):
" +------------------------------+--------+------+
" | Story Type                   | Points | Days |
" +------------------------------+--------+------+
" | Simple AMDP (1 table, agg)   | 3      | 4    |
" | Medium AMDP (multi-join)     | 5      | 7    |
" | Medium + CDS table function  | 8      | 10   |
" | Complex multi-method AMDP    | 13     | 15   |
" +------------------------------+--------+------+
" Velocity assumption: 1 point = ~1.3 days
" (includes design, dev, test, doc)

" Adjustment factors:
" Team new to SQLScript: +2 points
" No HANA DB Explorer: +1 point
" Strict SLA testing: +1-2 points
" CDS DCL auth needed: +1 point

followupAnswerBank.question: What is a performance baseline and why is it needed before estimating?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Performance baseline ek quantified measurement hai current system performance ka before any optimization — SE30 ke through captured runtime, ST05 ke through captured rows transferred, aur memory usage. Estimation se pehle yeh essential hai kyunki yeh problem ka actual scale reveal karta hai. 30-second runtime ko 10-minute runtime se different effort chahiye. Baseline acceptance criteria bhi set karta hai: post-optimization, humein in numbers ke against improvement demonstrate karna padta hai. Baseline ke bina effort estimate guesswork hai aur success objectively measure nahi ho sakti.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: A performance baseline is the quantified measurement of current system performance before any optimization — runtime captured via SE30, rows transferred captured via ST05, and memory usage. It is essential before estimation because it reveals the actual scale of the problem. A 30-second runtime needs different effort than a 10-minute runtime. The baseline also sets the acceptance criteria: post-optimization, we must demonstrate improvement against these numbers. Without baseline, effort estimate is guesswork and success cannot be objectively measured.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Manager ne bola 'report slow hai, optimize karo.' Baseline se pata chala: runtime 45 seconds, 10,000 rows transfer. Yeh actually acceptable tha — SLA 60 seconds thi. Optimization unnecessary tha. Baseline ne ek unnecessary project save kiya. Conversely, doosre case mein baseline 8 minutes tha — AMDP immediately justified.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Baseline capture kiye bina directly AMDP develop karna — do problems: nahi pata tha kitna improvement aaya, aur kabhi kabhi optimization zaruri hi nahi thi. Baseline pehle karo — measure twice, optimize once.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" How to capture performance baseline:

" 1. SE30 - Runtime Analysis
"    Transaction SE30
"    Variant -> Full workload
"    Run the report/transaction to be optimized
"    Note: Total runtime, DB time, ABAP time breakdown

" 2. ST05 - SQL Trace
"    Transaction ST05 -> Activate Trace
"    Run the report
"    ST05 -> Deactivate -> Display Trace
"    Note: Number of DB calls, rows fetched per call
"    KEY METRIC: total rows fetched to app server

" 3. Record baseline:
" +------------------------+----------+
" | Metric                 | Value    |
" +------------------------+----------+
" | Total Runtime (SE30)   | 8 min    |
" | DB Time %              | 20%      |
" | ABAP Time %            | 78%      |
" | Rows fetched (ST05)    | 500,000  |
" +------------------------+----------+
" -> This baseline drives solution choice AND effort

amdp-hana-018.easyUnderstanding: Simple words mein samjho: SAP mein transactions woh shortcuts hain jo directly kisi tool pe le jaate hain — jaise SE38 ABAP editor ke liye, SM30 table maintenance ke liye. AMDP aur HANA Optimization ke kaam mein bhi specific transactions use hote hain. Performance check ke liye ST05 aur SE30, code develop karne ke liye SE24 (class builder), database objects dekhne ke liye SE11, SQL queries directly run karne ke liye DBACOCKPIT. Yeh sab transactions daily kaam ke tools hain. Interviewer yeh poochh ke check karta hai ki tumhe actual kaam ka experience hai ya sirf theory pata hai.
amdp-hana-018.hinglishMasterExplanation:
AMDP aur HANA Optimization ke saath commonly used SAP transactions ka deep knowledge demonstrate karo:

1. SE24 — Class Builder: AMDP classes yahan develop hoti hain. Class create karo, IF_AMDP_MARKER_HDB add karo, method define karo, IMPLEMENTATION mein BY DATABASE PROCEDURE likhna padta hai. Activation yahan se hoti hai.

2. SE11 — ABAP Dictionary: AMDP ke IMPORTING/EXPORTING parameters ke liye DDIC table types yahan define karte hain. Structure types, table types, transparent table definitions yahan dekhte hain.

3. ST05 — SQL Trace: Performance baseline capture karne ke liye must-use. Trace on karo, report run karo, trace off karo, display karo — dikhaata hai exactly kitni DB calls gayi, kitni rows fetched hui, kaunsi tables hit hui. AMDP ke baad rows dramatically kam honi chahiye.

4. SE30 — Runtime Analysis (ABAP Trace): Total runtime, DB time vs ABAP time breakdown. AMDP se ABAP time kam honi chahiye, DB time ka share badhna chahiye — pushdown ka evidence.

5. DBACOCKPIT — Database Administration Cockpit: HANA directly access karne ke liye. SQL Console yahan hai — directly SQLScript run kar sakte ho. Performance monitoring, table statistics, expensive SQL statements dekhna. AMDP develop karte waqt SQLScript pehle yahan test karo.

6. HANA Studio / SAP HANA Database Explorer (Eclipse plugin ya web): AMDP ke peeche bana stored procedure directly yahan dekhte hain. Debug karte hain. SQLScript prototype karte hain pehle.

7. SE80 — Object Navigator: Project ka overall class structure dekho, objects browse karo.

8. SM37 — Background Job Monitor: Performance test ke liye AMDP wali report background mein run karke runtime measure karo.

9. ST12 — ABAP Trace with Statistics: More detailed than SE30 — method level granularity, AMDP method call time specifically dekh sakte ho.

10. AL11 — SAP Directories: Agar HANA results file mein dump kar rahe ho debugging ke liye.

11. CDS Views Development: ADT (Eclipse ABAP Development Tools) — CDS DDL files yahan create hote hain, F8 se data preview, @AccessControl DCL files yahan banate hain. AMDP ke saath CDS table function bhi ADT mein banate hain.

12. SAML (Solution Manager): Technical specifications aur change management link karne ke liye — enterprise projects mein.
